app.title: Linux Distro Picker
app.description: A distro chooser for beginners with a quiz
app.noResults: No distributions match your selected criteria.

gaming-ready.name: Gaming Ready
gaming-ready.description: Optimized for gaming with special software preinstalled

traditional.name: Traditional
traditional.description: Point releases with freezed software versions

mint.description: Been here for quite a while. Community-driven, stable and familiar. The main edition features the Cinnamon desktop environment. A general-purpose distro, can be used for gaming too (like most distros), but it doesn't come with special tools pre-installed. Works well even on 10-15 years old computers (with an SSD). If your computer has an HDD or just 2Gb of RAM (potentially Windows 7 era), use the Xfce version of Linux Mint.

kubuntu.description: A well-established and trusted distro, based on Ubuntu with the KDE Plasma desktop environment (the most feature-rich). It is backed by a huge corporation, Canonical. Similar hardware requirements to Linux Mint.

zorin.description: A polished distro focused on visual appeal and ease of use, featuring the Gnome desktop environment with 3D animations and various out-of-the-box layouts. Similar hardware requirements to Linux Mint.

popos.description: Built by System76, featuring the COSMIC desktop environment (macOS-like) and focusing on usability. Has tiling window support. Good for creative work and productivity, but not an ideal choice for gaming, as its package versions considerably lag behind their latest releases and the COSMIC desktop environment is relatively new.

kde-linux.description: An immutable (using Btrfs snapshots) Linux OS built by KDE. Immutability makes it extremely stable, but keeps the system read-only (which isn't a problem for most users). Strives to be as user-friendly as possible. Yet to be released, here just for information.

fedora.description: Perfect for developers. Comes in many editions/spins, including one with the KDE Plasma desktop environment. Fedora is a semi-rolling distribution (meaning the apps get frequent updates, but the base system remains unchanged until the next big release), making it both stable and up-to-date.

endeavouros.description: Arch Linux made easy. It provides a graphical installer and makes minimal changes to the base system. Doesn't configure zram/swap by default.

cachyos.description: An Arch-based rolling distribution optimized for performance, featuring the KDE Plasma desktop environment (among many others). Though not initially gaming-ready, it can be easily set up for gaming by installing a single meta package (cachyos-gaming-meta). The handheld edition, on the other hand, is gaming-ready. Perfect for tech enthusiasts who want to have full control over their system. The extreme optimizations of CachyOS may result in less stability compared to EndeavourOS.

steamos.description: An official gaming OS from Valve that powers Steam Deck, Steam Machine and other devices like Lenovo Legion Go. Immutable (using its own RAUC and desync technologies), user-friendly and optimized for gaming. Features the KDE Plasma desktop environment. Released for handhelds, but yet to be released for desktops.

bazzite.description: A gaming immutable (using OSTree) Linux distribution, perfect for both PCs and handhelds (has a special edition for them). A SteamOS alternative. Take a survey on the official website to see if your hardware supports the Linux gaming.

nobara.description: A gaming distro with a mutable core system, giving you full control. It was developed by the creator of Proton-GE, a utility at the heart of Linux gaming, and features both PC and handheld versions. Provides you the full power of Fedora, but comes with graphics drivers and gaming software preinstalled on top of that.

pikaos.description: A mutable gaming distribution. Similar to Nobara, but based on Debian (unstable branch with newer software). It offers you the option to install a gaming meta-package after the OS installation, meaning you can keep it bloat-free and configure it yourself. The Gnome edition features a layout switcher, just like Zorin OS.

lubuntu.description: A user-friendly lightweight Ubuntu variant, perfect for computers produced between 2004 and 2011 designed for Windows XP/Vista with 1 or 2Gb of RAM.

antiX.description: Ultra light distro, requires as minimum as 256Mb of RAM to run (more to run apps smoothly). Suitable for ancient computers designed for Windows XP.

additionalInfo.title: Additional Information
additionalInfo.content:
This app doesn't include other popular distributions (like, Arch Linux, Ubuntu, Debian, openSUSE, NixOS, Gentoo, Void, Artix, Slackware, MX Linux, Omarchy, Manjaro, elementaryOS, Solus, and so on), enterprise-level distributions (like, Red Hat, AlmaLinux, Rocky Linux) and niche distributions (like, Kali, Alpine) as it's intended for complete beginners and aims to simplify the choice. In the end of the day, most Linux distributions share (distribute) the same technologies and are very similar.

As of mid-2026, there are approximately 100-120 million Linux users worldwide (around 30 million of whom use Ubuntu, especially in the corporate sector). This represents about 4% of all PC users.

Hints for dual-booting: install Linux on a separate disk and don't use Windows tools to resize partitions on it (even non-Linux ones) as Windows can make Linux unbootable.

Software that I recommend.

Browsers: Brave, Helium, Zen.
Office: OnlyOffice, LibreOffice.
Torrent client: qBittorrent.
Disk analyzer: Filelight (KDE/Qt), Baobab (Gnome/GTK), gdu (command-line written in Rust).
Local AI: LM Studio (I recommend Mistral model).
Extended software support: Flatpak (Bazaar or your software center), Distrobox (DistroShelf).
Running Windows apps: Bottles, Winboat.
Cleaners: BleachBit, Stacer (not needed for immutable systems).
System Backup: Timeshift (not needed for immutable systems).
Nvidia Shadow Play alternative: GPU Screen Recorder.
Photoshop alternatives: Photoshop online, Photopea, GIMP, Krita, Affinity (via Bottles), Canva (for simple tasks).
Lightroom alternative: Darktable.
Illustrator alternative: Inkscape.
Premier Pro alternative: DaVinci Resolve, Kdenlive.
Ventoy for creating a bootable USB drive with multiple Linux distributions.

question.text: How would you like to use the distribution?

answers.text: I'm lazy, just tell me what to use

answers.result: Linux Mint, Kubuntu, or Zorin OS—depending on which design you like more.
Pop!_OS. Zorin OS also offers a macOS-like layout in its paid version.

answers.text: I want it to be preconfigured for gaming

question.text: Do you want to be able to edit system files?

answers.result: Bazzite. The distribution is available for both PCs and handhelds. You can also go with SteamOS if it supports your device (it doesn't officially support PCs yet). A mutable gaming distro is also a nice choice.
Nobara or PikaOS. With their mutable systems, you'll be able to tweak system files and install any packages you want. If you're technically skilled enough, you can also go with CachyOS.

answers.text: I want to develop on it
answers.result: Fedora is your ideal choice. It's both stable and offers up-to-date software. Plus, you can choose from various desktop environments to suit your preferences. That said, distros like Linux Mint, Kubuntu, Zorin OS, Pop!_OS or EndeavourOS are also viable options.

answers.text: I'm a tech enthusiast and want to have full control over my system
answers.result: Sounds like CachyOS was made for you. Or, if you want something even closer to barebones Arch, EndeavourOS is worth checking out.

answers.text: I want to recover my old PC with it
Not extremely old, but it feels sluggish
It is truly ancient (2004 or older) or has just 1Gb of RAM
I want to install it on a device with an Apple Silicon processor

share.title: Enjoying the app?
share.text: Help others discover Linux by sharing this app with your friends!
